title: Bhikkhus, I eat only one meal a day. Bhikkhus, by eating only one meal a day, I feel free from disease, free from affliction, lightness,…
content: Bhikkhus, I eat only one meal a day. Bhikkhus, by eating only one meal a day, I feel free from disease, free from affliction, lightness, strength, and a pleasant abiding. Bhikkhus, you too should eat only one meal a day. Bhikkhus, by eating only one meal a day, you too will feel free from disease, free from affliction, lightness, strength, and a pleasant abiding.

title: - It is wonderful, Venerable Kaccāna, it is marvelous, Venerable Kaccāna, how Venerable Kaccāna has defined non-guarding of the faculties. Guarding of the faculties, guarding…
content: - It is wonderful, Venerable Kaccāna, it is marvelous, Venerable Kaccāna, how Venerable Kaccāna has defined non-guarding of the faculties. Guarding of the faculties, guarding of the faculties, Venerable Kaccāna has said. To what extent, Venerable Kaccāna, is there guarding of the faculties?" - "Here, brahmin, when a bhikkhu sees a form with the eye, his mind does not incline toward agreeable forms nor recoil from disagreeable forms; he lives with mindfulness established, with an immeasurable mind. He truly understands the liberation of mind and liberation by wisdom. Therefore, the evil, unwholesome states that have arisen are completely abandoned without remainder. ... when he hears a sound ... smells an odor ... tastes a taste ... feels a touch ... when he cognizes a mind-object with the mind, his mind does not incline toward agreeable mind-objects nor recoil from disagreeable mind-objects; he lives with mindfulness established, with an immeasurable mind. He truly understands the liberation of mind and liberation by wisdom. Therefore, the evil, unwholesome states that have arisen are completely abandoned without remainder. Thus, brahmin, is guarding of the faculties.

title: Whereas some samaṇas and brahmins, while enjoying food offered by the faithful, still live maintaining themselves by wrong livelihood such as: arranging auspicious days for…
content: Whereas some samaṇas and brahmins, while enjoying food offered by the faithful, still live maintaining themselves by wrong livelihood such as: arranging auspicious days for wedding or betrothal, selecting favorable times for reconciliation, selecting favorable times for estrangement, selecting favorable times for debt collection, selecting favorable times for borrowing or spending, using charms to bring good luck, using charms to cause misfortune, using charms to induce abortion, using charms to make the tongue stick, using charms to lock the jaw, using charms to make the hand drop, using charms to cause deafness; consulting mirrors, consulting mediums, consulting girls, consulting devas for omens; worshipping the sun, worshipping the great earth, blowing fire, invoking the goddess of fortune. Samaṇa Gotama abstains from such wrong livelihood. — Bhikkhus, these are the praises of the Tathāgata by ordinary people.

title: Here, bhikkhus, a bhikkhu has faith, virtue, learning, energy, and wisdom. Whatever the faithless fear, bhikkhus, that does not frighten the faithful. Therefore, this quality…
content: Here, bhikkhus, a bhikkhu has faith, virtue, learning, energy, and wisdom. Whatever the faithless fear, bhikkhus, that does not frighten the faithful. Therefore, this quality is a non-fearful quality for a learner. Whatever the immoral fear, bhikkhus, that does not frighten the virtuous. Therefore, this quality is a non-fearful quality for a learner. Whatever the ignorant fear, bhikkhus, that does not frighten the learned. Therefore, this quality is a non-fearful quality for a learner. Whatever the lazy fear, bhikkhus, that does not frighten the energetic. Therefore, this quality is a non-fearful quality for a learner. Whatever the evil-witted fear, bhikkhus, that does not frighten the wise. Therefore, this quality is a non-fearful quality for a learner.

title: And what, friends, is that Middle Way — (the way) that gives rise to (pure) vision, gives rise to (true) knowledge, leading to peace, direct…
content: And what, friends, is that Middle Way — (the way) that gives rise to (pure) vision, gives rise to (true) knowledge, leading to peace, direct knowledge, enlightenment, Nibbāna? It is the Noble Eightfold Path, namely: Right View, Right Intention, Right Speech, Right Action, Right Livelihood, Right Effort, Right Mindfulness, Right Concentration. Friends, this is that Middle Way, giving rise to (pure) vision, giving rise to (true) knowledge, leading to peace, direct knowledge, enlightenment, Nibbāna.

title: For one who is born a human, When the good Dhamma is taught, If they do not seize the opportunity, They pass beyond the moment.…
content:
For one who is born a human,
When the good Dhamma is taught,
If they do not seize the opportunity,
They pass beyond the moment.
Many inopportune times are spoken of
That obstruct human beings.
Only five or ten calamities [occur],
The Tathāgata appears in the world.
To encounter him face to face
Is very hard to find in the world.
To be born as a human
And for the good Dhamma to be taught
Is enough for one to strive
For whoever desires their welfare.
How could one, knowing the good Dhamma,
Let the opportunity pass by!
Whoever lets the opportunity pass
Grieves in hell.
Here, whoever abandons
The fixed course of the good Dhamma,
Like a merchant who loses goods,
Will grieve for a long time.
One covered by ignorance,
Transgressing the good Dhamma,
Must endure for a long time
Wandering in birth and death.
Whoever is born as a human
At a time when the Dhamma is well taught,
Whether past, present, or future,
Acts according to the Teacher's advice,
Seizes the opportunity in this world
For the unsurpassed holy life.
Whoever has entered the path
Proclaimed by the Tathāgata,
Whoever lives restrained
As taught by the One with the Eye of Dhamma,
As declared by the Kinsman of the Sun,
Let them dwell always mindful,
Guarding, without greed for sensual pleasures,
Cutting off all underlying tendencies,
Not running into Māra's realm.
They, in this world,
Reach the far shore,
And they also attain
The destruction of the taints.

title: Few are those among people Who have reached the far shore. The rest of the populace Run back and forth on this shore. Those who…
content:
Few are those among people
Who have reached the far shore.
The rest of the populace
Run back and forth on this shore.

Those who follow the teaching,
The Dhamma well proclaimed,
They will reach the far shore,
Crossing Māra's realm so hard to cross.

Having abandoned dark states,
The wise one cultivates bright states,
Leaving home for homelessness,
In seclusion, hard to delight in.

There, desiring delight,
Abandon all sensual desires,
The wise one purifies himself,
His mind of defilements.

Those who develop well,
Their minds in the enlightenment factors,
Abandoning all attachments,
Delighting in non-clinging,
With taints destroyed, radiant,
They attain Nibbāna in this very life.

title: And, bhikkhus, what is the Noble Truth of the Origin of Suffering? With ignorance as condition, volitional formations arise; with volitional formations as condition, consciousness;…
content:
And, bhikkhus, what is the Noble Truth of the Origin of Suffering?
With ignorance as condition, volitional formations arise; with volitional formations as condition, consciousness; with consciousness as condition, name-and-form; with name-and-form as condition, the six sense bases; with the six sense bases as condition, contact; with contact as condition, feeling; with feeling as condition, craving; with craving as condition, clinging; with clinging as condition, existence; with existence as condition, birth; with birth as condition, aging, death, sorrow, lamentation, pain, grief, and despair arise. Thus is the origination of this whole mass of suffering. This, bhikkhus, is called the Noble Truth of the Origin of Suffering.

title: Not attached to anything, they wander in the world, Always mindful, abandoning the view of 'mine' — At the proper time offer them the sacrifice.…
content:
Not attached to anything, they wander in the world,
Always mindful, abandoning the view of 'mine' —
At the proper time offer them the sacrifice.
The brahmin seeking merit, give thus.

One who has abandoned sensual pleasures, self-controlled, wandering,
Who knows the end of birth and death,
Peaceful, cooled like a lake —
The Tathāgata is worthy of the sacrificial offering.

Equal to the equal, avoiding the unequal,
The Tathāgata has attained limitless wisdom,
Unstained, undefiled, in this world or the next —
The Tathāgata is worthy of the sacrificial offering.

title: The hunter set his snare, The wise deer does not approach the trap. Having eaten the crop, we go our way, Leaving the deer-hunters to…
content:
The hunter set his snare,
The wise deer does not approach the trap.
Having eaten the crop, we go our way,
Leaving the deer-hunters to lament.
